section.title: God Is King over All the Earth
section.sectionRationale: An enthronement psalm summoning all peoples, not Israel alone, to celebrate God's universal kingship, its picture of God ascending amid shouts of joy has long been read by the church alongside Christ's own ascension and reign over all nations.
section.thesis: All peoples are summoned to clap and shout for God the great King who reigns over all the earth and gathers the nations to Himself, a psalm fulfilled in Christ's ascension to His throne and the ingathering of the nations as fellow heirs with Abraham's people.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the summons to all peoples to shout for the great King, verses 1-4; God's ascension amid joyful acclamation, verses 5-7; and God's universal reign over the gathered nations, verses 8-9.

units.label: Clap your hands, all you peoples
units.note: the summons to all peoples to shout for the great King

verses.text: Clap your hands, all you peoples; shout unto God with a voice of triumph.
How {{yare|awesome}} is the LORD Most High, the great King over all the earth!
He subdues nations beneath us, and peoples under our feet.
He chooses our inheritance for us, the pride of Jacob, whom He loves. Selah

units.label: God has ascended amid shouts of joy

verses.text: God has {{alah|ascended}} amid shouts of joy, the LORD with the sound of the horn.
Sing praises to God, sing praises; sing praises to our King, sing praises!
For God is King of all the earth; sing profound praises to Him.

units.label: God reigns over the nations
units.note: God's universal reign over the gathered nations

verses.text: God reigns over the nations; God is seated on His holy throne.
The nobles of the nations have assembled as the people of the God of Abraham; for the shields of the earth belong to God; He is highly exalted.

yare.gloss: Awesome, to be feared or held in reverent awe. The psalmist uses this word to describe the LORD Most High, the great King whose reign inspires reverence in every people, not Israel alone.

alah.gloss: To go up or ascend. God's ascension amid shouts of joy and the sound of the horn has long been read by the church alongside Psalm 68:18 and Christ's own ascension, quoted in Ephesians 4:8-10.

basic.context: This enthronement psalm summons all peoples, not Israel alone, to clap and shout for God, the great King over all the earth, who has chosen Israel's inheritance and now ascends amid joyful acclamation to His holy throne, gathering the nobles of the nations as the people of the God of Abraham.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm's universal summons, 'all you peoples,' verse 1, and its picture of the nations gathered 'as the people of the God of Abraham,' verse 9, reveal that God's kingship was never meant for Israel alone but always aimed at drawing every nation into worship of the one true King.
basic.originalLanguages: The LORD is called yare, awesome, verse 2, the great King over all the earth. He is pictured as alah, ascending, verse 5, amid shouts of joy and the sound of the horn, language the church has long read alongside Christ's own ascension.
basic.typology: God's ascension amid shouts of joy and the sound of the horn anticipates Christ's ascension to the Father's right hand, celebrated in Acts 1 and interpreted in Ephesians 4:8-10, while the nobles of the nations gathering as Abraham's people anticipates Gentile believers grafted into God's covenant family through faith.
basic.christ: This psalm's picture of God ascending amid triumphant shouts finds its fulfillment in Christ's ascension, Acts 1:9-11, interpreted by Paul in Ephesians 4:8-10 as the King's triumphant rise to give gifts to His church, while the psalm's ingathering of the nations anticipates the church, Jew and Gentile united as Abraham's true offspring, Galatians 3:29.
basic.god: God is shown as the great King over all the earth, not merely Israel's tribal deity, One whose subjugation of nations and choice of inheritance for His people ultimately serves His larger purpose of drawing every nation to worship Him.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 4:8-10 interprets Christ's ascension in the same triumphant, gift-giving terms this psalm's enthronement language anticipates. Acts 1:9-11 records Christ's actual ascension to the Father's right hand. Galatians 3:26-29 shows Gentile believers becoming Abraham's true offspring through faith, fulfilling this psalm's vision of gathered nations.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's summons for every people to shout for God's universal kingship, and its vision of the nations gathered as Abraham's people, finds its fulfillment in Christ's ascension to His throne and in the church, Jew and Gentile together, gathered as the true people of the God of Abraham through faith in Him.

context.literary: An enthronement psalm of the sons of Korah, structured in three brief movements moving from universal summons, verses 1-4, to God's ascension amid acclamation, verses 5-7, to His settled reign over the gathered nations, verses 8-9.
context.historical: Likely sung at a festival celebrating God's kingship, possibly connected to the ark's procession into the sanctuary or an annual celebration of God's enthronement, its universal scope, addressing 'all you peoples,' reaching beyond Israel's own borders.
context.canonical: One of the clearest Old Testament expressions of God's kingship over all nations, not Israel alone, anticipating the New Testament's vision of the gospel going out to every people and the church gathered from every nation as one people of God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To summon every people, not Israel alone, to recognize and celebrate God's universal kingship, and to picture His enthronement as drawing the nations themselves into worship alongside Abraham's own people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific liturgical celebration of God's kingship, likely tied to a festival procession; it prescribes as an abiding pattern universal praise for God's reign and anticipates the ingathering of all nations into His one worshiping people.

debates.question: Does 'God has ascended' in verse 5 describe a historical liturgical procession, such as the ark being carried up to Zion, or point forward typologically to a future divine ascension?

views.label: The verse describes a historical or liturgical reenactment of the ark of the covenant being carried up into the sanctuary on Mount Zion, the concrete occasion for this psalm's composition
views.case: The sound of the horn and the shouts of joy fit the description elsewhere in Scripture of the ark's procession, 2 Samuel 6:15, suggesting a real liturgical event as the psalm's original setting.

views.label: The verse points forward typologically beyond any ark procession to God's own final ascension and enthronement over all nations, later fulfilled in Christ's ascension
views.case: The psalm's universal scope, God ascending as King 'of all the earth,' verse 7, and gathering 'the nobles of the nations,' verse 9, exceeds what any single ark procession could accomplish, pointing toward a greater fulfillment.

debates.bearing: Whichever original liturgical setting is in view, the church has long read this ascending King as foreshadowing Christ's own ascension and enthronement over all nations, a reading the New Testament's use of similar enthronement psalms, such as Psalm 68, supports.

study.originalLanguages: The LORD is called yare, awesome, verse 2, the great King over all the earth, His reign commanding reverent awe from every people. He is pictured as alah, ascending, verse 5, amid shouts of joy and the sound of the horn, language read by the church alongside Christ's own triumphant ascension.

typology.type: God ascending amid shouts of joy and the sound of the horn, verse 5
typology.fulfillment: Christ's ascension to the right hand of the Father, Acts 1:9-11, interpreted by Paul as the triumphant King's rise, Ephesians 4:8-10.

typology.type: The nobles of the nations gathered as the people of the God of Abraham, verse 9
typology.fulfillment: Gentile believers grafted in as Abraham's true offspring through faith in Christ, Galatians 3:29, Ephesians 2:11-19.

study.christ: This psalm's vision of God ascending amid triumphant shouts and the sound of the horn is fulfilled in Christ's own ascension, recorded in Acts 1:9-11 and interpreted by Paul in Ephesians 4:8-10 as the victorious King ascending on high to give gifts to His people. The psalm's closing picture of the nobles of the nations gathered as the people of the God of Abraham anticipates the church, drawn from every nation, made one new people through faith in Christ, Galatians 3:26-29.
study.god: God is revealed as King not merely of Israel but of all the earth, whose reign, though it chose Israel's own inheritance, was always aimed at gathering every nation into worship of Him alone, He who is highly exalted above every earthly power.

crossReferences.note: Interprets Christ's ascension as the triumphant King's rise to give gifts to His church, echoing this psalm's enthronement language.
Records Christ's actual ascension to the Father's right hand, the historical fulfillment this psalm's imagery anticipates.
Shows Gentile believers becoming Abraham's true offspring through faith, fulfilling this psalm's vision of gathered nations.

study.oneStory: This psalm's summons for every people to celebrate God's universal kingship, and its vision of the nations gathered as Abraham's people, finds its fulfillment in Christ's ascension to His throne and in the church, drawn from every nation, gathered as the one true people of the God of Abraham through faith in Him.

quiz.q: Who is summoned to clap and shout in verse 1?

options.text: All peoples, not Israel alone.
options.note: The psalm's universal summons addresses every people, revealing God's kingship extends beyond Israel.

options.text: Only the priests serving in the temple.
options.note: The summons is explicitly to 'all you peoples,' not a restricted group of priests.

options.text: Only the king and his royal court.
options.note: The call goes out universally to all peoples, not merely to royal officials.

options.text: Only the tribe of Judah.
options.note: The summons reaches beyond any single tribe to all peoples of every nation.

quiz.q: What title does verse 2 give the LORD?

options.text: The great King over all the earth.
options.note: This title establishes God's universal kingship as the psalm's central theme.

options.text: The God of Israel's armies alone.
options.note: The title given is universal, King over all the earth, not restricted to Israel's armies.

options.text: The judge of Israel only.
options.note: The psalm's scope is explicitly universal, King over all the earth, not Israel alone.

options.text: The shepherd of Jacob's flock.
options.note: While God does care for Jacob, verse 2's specific title is King over all the earth.

options.text: God ascends amid shouts of joy and the sound of the horn.
options.note: This ascension imagery has long been read by the church alongside Christ's own triumphant ascension.

options.text: God descends to judge the nations directly.
options.note: The verse describes God ascending, not descending, amid joyful acclamation.

options.text: The ark of the covenant is destroyed by enemies.
options.note: The verse celebrates a triumphant ascension, not destruction or defeat.

options.text: The people mourn the loss of their king.
options.note: The scene is one of joyful shouting and celebration, not mourning.

quiz.q: Who gathers in verse 9 as the people of the God of Abraham?

options.text: The nobles of the nations.
options.note: This pictures nations beyond Israel being drawn into God's own covenant people, anticipating Gentile inclusion in Christ.

options.text: Only the descendants of Isaac and Jacob.
options.note: The text specifically names the nobles of the nations, not merely Israel's own physical descendants.

options.text: The armies defeated in battle.
options.note: The nobles gather in worship and assembly, not as a defeated or subjugated army.

options.text: The priests of foreign religions.
options.note: The nobles gather as worshipers of the one true God, not as representatives of other religions.

quiz.q: What New Testament event does this psalm's ascension language anticipate?

options.text: Christ's ascension to the Father's right hand.
options.note: Acts 1:9-11 and Ephesians 4:8-10 present Christ's ascension in triumphant, kingly terms this psalm anticipates.

options.text: The birth of Christ in Bethlehem.
options.note: The ascension imagery points toward Christ's rising to His throne, not His birth.

options.text: The destruction of the Jerusalem temple.
options.note: The psalm's ascension imagery is triumphant and hopeful, not connected to the temple's destruction.

options.text: The final judgment at the end of the age.
options.note: While God's universal reign does anticipate final judgment, the specific ascension imagery of verse 5 points most directly to Christ's own ascension.

questions: This psalm summons all peoples, not Israel alone, to worship God as King. How does knowing that God's kingship has always aimed at the nations shape your own sense of mission toward people different from you?
God is described as awesome, the great King over all the earth. When was the last time you felt genuine awe, rather than mere familiarity, in worship?
God's ascension amid shouts of joy anticipates Christ's own ascension to His throne. How does picturing Christ enthroned and reigning right now change how you face today's uncertainties?

The nobles of the nations gather as the people of the God of Abraham, verse 9, anticipating Gentile inclusion in Christ. If you came to faith from outside Israel's physical line, how does this psalm's vision deepen your sense of belonging to God's people?

The psalm calls for repeated, enthusiastic praise, 'sing praises, sing praises,' verse 6. What would it look like for your own praise this week to move from routine to genuine celebration of God's reign?

translationNotes.note: Awesome renders yare, describing reverent fear or wonder appropriate before the great King over all the earth, not mere intellectual admiration.
Ascended renders alah, later read by the church at Ascension Day alongside Psalm 68:18 and Ephesians 4:8-10 concerning Christ's own rise to His throne.

The nobles of the nations gathered as the people of the God of Abraham can be read either as foreign rulers submitting to Israel's God or as the nations themselves being joined to Abraham's people; either reading pictures Gentile inclusion in God's worship.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 47:1-9. No em or en dashes present. Searched gospelinlife.com, truthforlife.org, and desiringgod.org this session for a dedicated Psalm 47 sermon from the trusted cluster; the only Truth For Life results covering this passage were a general two-part series spanning Ezra through Malachi, not a dedicated Psalm 47 message, so sermon status is none.
